readme.title: README
readme.description: Learn the purpose of SunCVE, what it offers, and who makes it possible

purpose.title: Why SunCVE exists
purpose.lead: SunCVE exists to answer what few tools answer: how a vulnerability is actually born and fixed inside the code. Instead of just listing CVEs, we analyze the commits, the diffs and the exploits of each flaw — linking the security advisory to the code that caused it and the code that fixed it.
purpose.challenge: It all started from a practical challenge: filtering vulnerabilities by popular repositories and by specific tags, something no database offered out of the box. Today SunCVE bridges two ecosystems — GitHub and WordPress — in one place, running entirely in your browser.
purpose.commitsText: We read commits to find where a flaw was introduced and where it was fixed.
purpose.diffsText: We compare the changes to understand the exact nature of each vulnerability.
purpose.exploitsText: We cross-reference public PoCs and exploits to measure the real impact of each CVE.
purpose.starsText: We filter by popular repositories and specific tags to focus on what matters.

offers.title: What SunCVE offers
offers.lead: Everything runs in your browser, with a local SQLite database loaded via sql.js. Nothing leaves your computer.
offers.cveSearchText: Search and filter vulnerabilities by CVE, vendor, product, and description.
offers.repoSearchText: Explore GitHub repositories and WordPress plugins linked to vulnerabilities.
offers.dashboardText: Overview with charts of CVEs by period, severity, and CWE trends.
offers.exploitsText: Spot CVEs with public exploitation code available.
offers.commitsText: Find the commits that fixed each vulnerability.
offers.ecosystemsText: Two ecosystems in one place, each with its own popularity metrics.

filters.title: Available filters
filters.lead: Combine the filters below to reach exactly the vulnerabilities you are looking for.
filters.severity: Severity: critical, high, medium, and low
filters.cwe: Individual CWE and by vulnerability category
filters.flags: Has exploit, has repository, and has fix commit
filters.ecosystem: Ecosystem: GitHub, WordPress, npm or Packagist
filters.datePeriod: Publication period, including a specific date

sources.title: Data sources
sources.lead: SunCVE is only possible because of these open projects. We are deeply grateful to each one of them for keeping this data accessible to the community.
sources.cvelistv5Text: Official CVE list in the CVE JSON 5.0 format, maintained by the CVE Project.
sources.advisoryText: Security advisory database curated by GitHub for many ecosystems.
sources.pocText: Collection of public proofs of concept and exploits associated with CVEs.
sources.wordpressText: rix4uni/wordpress-plugins dataset from the official WordPress directory, with active installs, downloads and last updated date.
sources.npmText: nice-registry/all-the-package-repos maps each npm package to its repository, cross-referenced with download counts from nice-registry/download-counts to enrich GitHub repos with package URL and downloads.
sources.packagistText: OSV Packagist export plus the Packagist API resolve each affected Composer package to its repository and total downloads, matched against the repositories from our references.

contributors.title: Contributors
contributors.lead: A special thank you to everyone who dedicates their time to SunCVE. You are the heart of this project.
contributors.errorTitle: We could not load the contributors right now
contributors.errorText: This can happen due to GitHub API rate limits. You can still see all of them directly in the repository.
contributors.thanks: Thank you for building SunCVE with us.
